roof_system: Primarily BUR/tar-and-gravel or modified bitumen, with possible coated sections on portions of the roof field

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Uniform granular, speckled roof surface across the main fields is consistent with a built-up or gravel-surfaced low-slope roof.
Flat low-slope roof appearance with a mottled mineral-looking surface could also fit modified bitumen from aerial view.
Some areas appear more uniform and lighter, but aerial resolution is too limited to confirm a coating; the surface could also be weathered membrane or gravel surfacing.

roof_age_estimate: Likely original or near-original era roof based on 1979 building date, but visible imagery alone cannot confirm installation date or major replacements.

notes: Localized darkened/stained areas are visible on the roof surface, including some irregular discoloration that may indicate ponding, wet insulation, prior repairs, or localized drainage issues.
Multiple rooftop penetrations and small rooftop elements are visible, increasing flashing and leak-maintenance risk on an older low-slope roof.
No clear tree overhang onto the roof is visible in the aerial image.
The 03/11/2018 - 03/17/2018 imagery may understate current deterioration.

observations: Low-slope commercial warehouse roof with multiple connected roof fields.
Roof surface is generally intact from aerial view but not uniformly clean; several darkened patches and tonal variations are visible.
Several penetrations/vents/roof-mounted elements are present, especially on the right-side roof field, which raises flashing and seal risk.
Some areas near roof edges and field transitions appear darker and possibly moisture-affected, though aerial imagery cannot confirm wet insulation.
No obvious major open breaches or severe widespread collapse are visible in the provided image.
summary: Aerial imagery of this 1979 industrial warehouse shows a low-slope roof that appears serviceable but only fair overall, with moderate risk driven by visible dark staining/discoloration, possible localized ponding or drainage-related moisture, and multiple penetrations that increase flashing and leak potential. The roof surface looks broadly intact, but the mottled appearance suggests recurring maintenance or water-management concerns on an older roof field. Because the 3
recommendation: Have a qualified commercial roofing contractor perform an onsite inspection with moisture testing where warranted, confirm the exact roof system and repair history, and evaluate flashing, seams, drainage, and any suspect stained areas. If the roof is otherwise structurally sound and the substrate is compatible, consider whether a silicone roof restoration is feasible before a more costly overlay or replacement; a properly designed silicone system can reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, create a seamless white reflective surface, improve UV and hail resistance, withstand ponding water when correctly installed, and may qualify for a manufacturer warranty of up to 20 years. If moisture damage, failed insulation, deck issues, or incompatible materials are found, pursue overlay or full replacement evaluation instead.
